item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the SCP in itself, but dislike Serapis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bordering on necropost, but I personally have upvoted but dislike the Serapis tie-in because Serapis is too final: it provides both a beginning and end for humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As is your right. Is it just the edits, or would you have downvoted regardless? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally disagree, and will downvote accordingly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made edits based on Kalinin's Serapis event storyline, because why not? I like the idea of stuff actually happening to other stuff. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fan art depicting this is very welcome. :p

Edit: if it actually was Big Bird, that implies Sesame Street Hitler is a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now imagine that the bird monster he's fighting is actually Big Bird from Sesame Street.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I like the "time travel to kill Hitler" angle. In addition to that idea being a dead horse for years at this point, I've always found it odd that everyone with access to time travel would leap to this conclusion. Hitler, in many ways, was a strategic asset to the Allies, because of his inept military leadership. Imagine an adherent of Nazi ideology from high up in the Army General Staff taking over, and how much worse things could have been.

The thing is, I like my Foundation pulp-y, and trying to kill Hitler sounds a lot more plausible in that setting (Abe Gustowski: Timetravellin' Nazi Killer!), but I can see where you're coming from. I guess it's a matter of context (which I'll grant you this article has none of, since it's presented as-is.)

Gustowski's dialogue is fine, but I feel like it could be fine-tuned just a little bit to add even more credence to the character. Little things; I feel like he says "son" a lot, the "badger's ass" thing is a little random, etc.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on the specifics of what to fix. Dialogue is always a weak point for me, so I'm glad I didn't F it up too hard this time. ;)

The story which inspires the title of your piece is thin. I would like to know more about this relationship, which developed under such unlikely circumstances and involves two incredibly strange people. But we don't even hear from one half of it, let alone about anything other than Gustowski cares about this person. Could be a great opportunity for an accompanying tale.

That is definitely a very good idea. And yeah, it's hard to develop that particular narrative in an article like this without either slapping readers in the face with it, or lengthening the article unbearably in order to drop subtle hints about their relationship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reinforced Anomalous Cadaver Locker (RACL)

I'll tell you right now we haven't seen the last of this reference. I'll see to that.

I'm neutral-voting this one. The attention to detail and the base concept are solid, and I did enjoy a lot of the article, and the underlying story of a strange relationship developing between a cranky old man and an interdimensional assassin (that sounds really stupid when I put it that way, but this avenue of writing about it isn't) is one that I can get behind.

There's a few dissonant elements, however. Granted, this is a successful article, so I'm not advocating carving this up by any stretch, but I think these are areas where improvement could happen:

  • I'm not sure I like the "time travel to kill Hitler" angle. In addition to that idea being a dead horse for years at this point, I've always found it odd that everyone with access to time travel would leap to this conclusion. Hitler, in many ways, was a strategic asset to the Allies, because of his inept military leadership. Imagine an adherent of Nazi ideology from high up in the Army General Staff taking over, and how much worse things could have been.
  • Gustowski's dialogue is fine, but I feel like it could be fine-tuned just a little bit to add even more credence to the character. Little things; I feel like he says "son" a lot, the "badger's ass" thing is a little random, etc.
  • The story which inspires the title of your piece is thin. I would like to know more about this relationship, which developed under such unlikely circumstances and involves two incredibly strange people. But we don't even hear from one half of it, let alone about anything other than Gustowski cares about this person. Could be a great opportunity for an accompanying tale.

It's solid work, but I feel like there's a measure of potential for this concept, that isn't quite being reached.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing is, they're not the focus of the article. I could certainly do a supplement page or something, because I agree there's lots of possibilities for weirdness and above all entertainment, but I'm going to leave the main article as-is. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made an SCP about travelling back in time to kill Hitler, and not only was it not a -J, but it was rather good. +1

One complaint, though. I was really expecting to get some examples of time travelers who manifested due to SCP-2451's effect, as most SCPs give examples. The lack of examples kinda confused me, and I think a log of subjects recovered from SCP-2451 could be rather entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminded me of Gordon Freeman's fictional thesis, titled Observation of Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen Entanglement on Supraquantum Structures by Induction Through Nonlinear Transuranic Crystal of Extremely Long Wavelength (ELW) Pulse from Mode-Locked Source Array.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't catch your edit…

EDIT: Also, I kinda want to tell -1 that there's a decent change that he did succeed in going back and killing Hitler; it's just that that created a separate reality instead of overwriting this one. Depending on the mechanics of temporal paradoxes in this particular reality construct, there would need to be a "baseline" reality where Hitler existed in order to create the impetus to send someone back to kill him.

I absolutely love it when people have far better theories and ideas about what's going on than I could ever have dreamt up. You, sir, have made my - so far pretty shitty - day. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP left m with a sour taste…. they kind only a good tale continuing the story can take care of
Congrats to the writer for such a fine SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hint: the person that made it started the thread. ;)

That aside, Scipper probably caches the Series pages to avoid having to call them again and again. Unfortunately this means it'll sometimes show incorrect titles. *shrug*

If you feel it should be addressed, feel free to bring it up in the thread you linked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have that Chrome plugin for the site that puts the title of the skip on its page. I'll find the thread for it - I can't remember who made it. For me, the page still has the incorrect spelling, even though it's fixed in the listing… that might be something to bring up in that thread about the plugin. I'll find the thread and edit this post.

E: Here it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, caught that one this morning and corrected it on the Series III page. Where are you seeing that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2451 - Love Trough Time, Space and Species

Shouldn't that be 'through'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's only in interviews that he's addressed as Mr. Gustowski.

Yeah, I should have clarified that.

I have a really hard time believing calling people by their XXXX designation is going to get any cooperation at all.

This is something I recently changed in my headcanon about the Foundation in general, and it's part of the reason I deleted my first 'successful' tale a few weeks back. I think people would benefit from adopting the more 'ethically oriented' concept of Foundation employees in general, but it might be hard for people to distinguish between clinical and dehumanizing when they're writing said characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad I'm not the only one who thinks this. I love it when this kind of thing happens in interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's still referred to as SCP-2451-1 in the official documentation. It's only in interviews that he's addressed as Mr. Gustowski. I have a really hard time believing calling people by their XXXX designation is going to get any cooperation at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the black void of despair is where the cookies are hidden!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The # format is weird, but whatevs. The guy's got personality, I like the idea of a spatial anomaly attached to a person at a fixed distance, and this is cool. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm kind of feeling like this is going to get downvotes simply because the guy is called "he" rather than "it."

This article is an excellent example of using clinical tone without saying that the Foundation employees all threw their souls into a black void of despair before getting hired. So much upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're absolutely right. They're gone. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I? :) What makes you say that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The biggest flaw is that SCP-2451-A and SCP-2451-B aren't clear at all, and they don't actually matter (in a story sense) by the end of the skip. It confused me a hell of a lot until I realized it didn't really matter for understanding, so I'd say try to clear it up or fix it or even just remove the subdesignations altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hitler's time travel exemption act with a twist.
And other-dimensional creatures also failing to kill their version of Hitler. Well done.
Also, Crayne, you seem to be very good at making fake book titles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So much +1.
Nice change up to get a good complex scp with a lot of moving parts and sub-plots after all the mini-skips from the contest, too. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your formatting in your special containment procedures are well, it feels like there is no better description for it.
But I didn't really understand the SCP itself, I have to reread the description carefully.
Therefor I'll give it an upvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reason for #14's containment situation earned my +1 on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really liking the shift towards more "pragmatic but not needlessly inhumane" Foundation. Especially for sapient, mostly harmless SCP's that might be willing to actively cooperate if they aren't being vivisected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this really, really clunky and bloated, with minimal payoff. Way too many subdivisions, all of which I forgot instantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kyle Reese and the T-800 at the same time? I find that as fantastic as Big Bird.

I'm in a good mood this morning, is what I'm saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little techno-babble-y, a little exposition-y, a bit long, but every part makes sense, and the payoff is worth it.

Also, I had a JROTC instructor who was a Lieutenant Colonel and I couldn't help but think of this guy as him. So now I'm imagining my instructor fighting an 8' bird monster, and that's fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I had absolutely nothing specific in mind, other than that she's not human. I am, however, adopting your headcanon. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon is that Ethel is a Terminator. Man, Skynet is/was/will be pissed when she doesn't/didn't/won't change history.

EDIT: Also, I kinda want to tell -1 that there's a decent change that he did succeed in going back and killing Hitler; it's just that that created a separate reality instead of overwriting this one. Depending on the mechanics of temporal paradoxes in this particular reality construct, there would need to be a "baseline" reality where Hitler existed in order to create the impetus to send someone back to kill him.

In short: time travel, temporal paradoxes, and the language needed to adequately describe both are all a pain in the brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks. And yeah, I'm kind of done with the whole stereotypical "you don't exist anymore, you are now an object" approach.

As for the other -2 specimens in containment, that was exactly what I hoped. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting story there…
What I did like best though is reading through the interviews at the end, and then scrolling back up to re-check the containment note for #14.

This Foundation with the somewhat higher ethical standards for treating the people under their containment-care is definitely growing on me more.

It does make me a little more curious about the version stored in the anomalous cadaver storage and the one who requires complete site evacuation on breach…

And then there's the detail on Dr Xyanks book not being available yet for at least another 8 years.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks for FortuneFavorsBold for looking this over.
